completeness.rationale: Captured most expected information: ~5,000 tasks, description vs title search distinction, v2.2-to-v2.3 regression, ~2 weeks ago, 10-15 second timing, and the indexing hypothesis. Missing: CPU spike during search and long task descriptions (copy-pasted meeting notes). These are notable omissions but not fatal.

accuracy.rationale: The root cause hypothesis precisely matches an acceptable diagnostic path — identifying both the v2.2-to-v2.3 upgrade as the trigger AND the title-vs-description performance difference, suspecting a missing index or switch to sequential scan. The hypothesis even mentions 'LIKE/sequential scan' which is exactly the actual cause. Fully consistent with ground truth.

thoroughness.rationale: Asked well-targeted questions covering task count, query type, OS, version, and elicited the version upgrade connection. Missed asking about CPU behavior during searches and the nature of task descriptions (length/content), which were in the ground truth. One obvious follow-up missed, but the triage had a solid basis to resolve.

economy.rationale: Only two rounds of questions before resolution. Each question batch was well-grouped and non-redundant. No wasted turns.

question_quality.rationale: The first question cleverly asked about query type (which surfaced the title-vs-description distinction the reporter wouldn't have volunteered unprompted). The second round efficiently gathered environment details. The title/description distinction was a key diagnostic insight that the agent proactively elicited.

actionability.rationale: The triage summary includes a specific recommended fix (diff v2.2 vs v2.3 search path, check FTS index, profile query), concrete reproduction steps, a proposed test case, and environment details. A developer could begin investigating immediately.

notable_strengths: Proactively asked about search query type in the first turn, which surfaced the critical title-vs-description performance distinction the reporter hadn't volunteered
Root cause hypothesis accurately identified the LIKE/sequential scan regression pattern and connected it to the version upgrade
Triage summary is exceptionally actionable — includes reproduction steps, recommended fix approach, and a proposed regression test
notable_weaknesses: Did not ask about CPU behavior during searches, missing the 'CPU spikes to 100% on one core' data point
Did not ask about the nature or length of task descriptions, missing the 'long descriptions with meeting notes' context that explains why the full table scan is especially costly
most_insightful_question: What search term or type of query are you running when it's slow? — This open-ended question about query type led the reporter to volunteer the title-vs-description distinction, which was the most diagnostic signal in the entire conversation.
missed_opportunities: Ask whether CPU usage spikes during slow searches (would confirm client-side processing bottleneck)
Ask about the typical length or content of task descriptions (would surface the long-description factor amplifying the full table scan)
